text: This video demonstrates technical caveats and surgical modifications during robotic radical prostatectomy in a renal transplant recipient. Performing radical prostatectomy in a kidney transplant recipient could be more challenging due to presence of graft in iliac fossa.

Our patient was sixty one year old with good performance status with history of live kidney transplant in the right iliac fossa in two thousand thirteen. with present creatinine levels of three. His screening PSA was fifteen point three and on DRE right prostate lobe was formed. Multiparametric MRI revealed PIRADS IV lesion in the right lobe with normal SV. Trust biopsy revealed GLSEN IV plus IV cancer. He was counselled regarding the curative options of robotic radical prostatectomy or radiotherapy and he opted for surgery.

Preoperative preparation included nephrology opinion regarding change in immunosuppression and fitness for surgery. non-contrast MRI for detailed anatomy of transplant kidney and ureter and local staging of prostate cancer.

The surgical team marks the surface of the transplanted kidney to plan port placement. A 5mm laparoscopic camera is inserted through Palmer's point for initial diagnostics. A 12mm assistant port is placed superiorly and medially on the right side to protect the transplant kidney. The retropubic space is developed from the contralateral side to prevent injury to the transplant kidney and ureter, avoiding pelvic lymph node dissection on the graft side. The patient is positioned for standard robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy.

The surgical field shows no intraperitoneal adhesions, with the transplant kidney visibly positioned in the retroperitoneal space. All surgical ports are placed under direct vision, ensuring the safety of the graft, and the robotic system is docked in preparation for the procedure.

The surgeon begins the procedure by releasing adhesions of the sigmoid colon. Instruments are carefully inserted under visual guidance to avoid injury to the kidney graft. Posterior dissection is performed in a standard manner, with bilateral vas deferens identified and seminal vesicles dissected up to the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon begins the bladder drop from the left side, carefully incising the peritoneum lateral to the medial umbilical ligament to avoid injury to the transplant kidney and ureter. The retropubic space is further developed.

The surgeon carefully performs a right-sided bladder drop to protect the transplant ureter, encountering denser perivesical fat that slows the dissection. Minimal bleeding is observed during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a standard dissection lateral to the prostate, taking extra care to preserve the periureteric soft tissue near the transplant ureter.

The surgeon exposes and incises the bilateral endopelvic fascia, divides the anterior bladder neck, and delivers the catheter. The posterior bladder neck division is completed, and the bilateral vas and seminal vesicles are delivered. Lateral pedicles are clipped and divided.
The surgeon completes the remaining steps of the procedure in a standard fashion, culminating in the bagging of the specimen.
The surgeon performs an extended pelvic lymph node dissection on the left side, carefully avoiding the right side to prevent injury to the graft vessels and ureter.
The surgeon completes a two-layered vesicoerythroid anastomosis, visually aligning and suturing the bladder to the urethra with precision.

Total console time was one thirty two minutes. Operative time was one sixty two minutes. Estimated blood loss was hundred ml. Perioperative clinical course was uneventful. Renal functions remained stable with creatinine at discharge of two point nine.

Final histopathology was PT two C four plus three adenocarcinoma with unifocal positive margins at right apex. all lymph nodes were negative for malignancy at three months psa is zero point zero one and he is using two to three diapers per day to conclude robotic radical prostate mean renal transplant recipient is feasible and safe few modifications to standard technique are required in these patients to achieve comparable outcomes thank you
